sections.title: Microsoft 365 Alerts
items: Multiple successful logins from different countries within 15 minutes

sections.title: Additional Findings
items: OAuth token remains active after password reset

questions.question: What MOST likely occurred?
options: Patch failure on the mail server
questions.explanation: Multiple successful logins from different countries, OAuth consent, MFA being enabled, and token persistence after password reset strongly suggest OAuth token/session abuse after phishing.

questions.question: Why did password reset not fully solve the issue?
options: Password resets do not affect firewall rules
OAuth tokens/app consent can remain valid after password changes
DNS cache entries preserve account access
Mailbox forwarding rules automatically reset passwords
questions.explanation: Password reset alone may not revoke already-granted OAuth permissions or active tokens, so attacker access can persist.

questions.question: What should be revoked FIRST?
questions.explanation: The malicious OAuth consent/token is the active access path and should be revoked directly.

questions.question: What logs would help investigation most?
options: OAuth consent logs, sign-in logs, mailbox audit logs, and forwarding-rule logs
questions.explanation: OAuth consent, sign-in, mailbox audit, and forwarding-rule logs can show how access was granted, where it was used from, and what mailbox activity occurred.

questions.question: What controls could reduce similar attacks?
options: Admin consent workflow, OAuth app restrictions, phishing training, and UEBA/impossible-travel detection
questions.explanation: Restricting OAuth consent, requiring admin approval, training users, and detecting anomalous sign-ins reduce the chance and impact of OAuth phishing.

sections.title: EDR Alerts
items: No malicious files found on disk

questions.question: What type of malware behavior is MOST likely occurring?
questions.explanation: Encoded PowerShell, suspicious outbound traffic, no malicious files on disk, and disappearance after reboot indicate memory-resident or fileless malware behavior.

questions.question: Why might antivirus fail to detect it?
options: Antivirus cannot detect any PowerShell activity
The malware may avoid disk-based signatures by running mainly in memory
HTTPS traffic prevents all endpoint monitoring
questions.explanation: Fileless or memory-resident malware may evade traditional disk-based signature detection because there may be little or no malicious file to scan.

questions.question: What forensic evidence should be collected FIRST?
questions.explanation: Because the malware disappears after reboot and appears memory-resident, volatile memory should be collected before power loss or reboot.

questions.question: What containment action is MOST appropriate?
options: Isolate the affected host from the network while preserving evidence
Ignore because no file was found
questions.explanation: Isolation limits C2/exfiltration while preserving the system state for investigation.

questions.question: What indicators strongly suggest memory-resident malware?
options: High RAM usage, no disk artifacts, encoded PowerShell, suspicious outbound traffic, and disappearance after reboot
questions.explanation: The combination of volatile behavior, lack of disk artifacts, encoded PowerShell, and outbound traffic strongly supports memory-resident malware.

sections.title: DNS Monitoring
items: Host WS-014 issued 2,900 DNS TXT queries in 45 minutes

sections.title: Network Evidence
items: No matching browser activity on WS-014
Outbound traffic is almost entirely UDP/53
Domain was registered less than 24 hours ago

questions.question: What activity is MOST likely occurring?
options: DNS tunneling or DNS-based data exfiltration
questions.explanation: High-volume TXT queries, Base64-like subdomains, a newly registered domain, and no browser activity strongly indicate DNS tunneling or DNS-based exfiltration.

questions.question: Which evidence most strongly supports exfiltration rather than normal DNS use?
options: The host has a valid DHCP lease
The DNS queries use long encoded-looking subdomains repeatedly
The workstation is joined to the domain
questions.explanation: Long encoded-looking subdomains repeated at high volume are unusual for normal DNS and commonly used to carry data in DNS tunneling.

questions.question: What should the analyst do FIRST to contain the threat?
options: Block the suspicious domain and isolate the affected host
Disable all internal DNS resolution permanently
questions.explanation: Blocking the domain limits the communication path, while isolating WS-014 prevents continued exfiltration or command-and-control activity.

questions.question: Which logs would help determine the internal source and scope?
options: DNS query logs, EDR telemetry, proxy logs, and DHCP mappings
questions.explanation: DNS logs show the queries, DHCP maps IP to host, EDR shows local process context, and proxy/log telemetry helps scope related outbound activity.

questions.question: Which SIEM detection would be MOST useful for this behavior?
options: Alert on any single DNS lookup
Alert on high-frequency DNS TXT queries with long/high-entropy subdomains to rare domains
Alert only when DNS is disabled
questions.explanation: A useful detection combines query type, frequency, entropy/length, and domain rarity to reduce false positives while catching tunneling behavior.

sections.title: Authentication Logs
items: 4625 failed logon events against multiple admin accounts

sections.title: Endpoint and SMB Activity
items: svc_backup has no normal history of interactive access

questions.question: What attack behavior is MOST likely occurring?
options: Credential compromise followed by lateral movement
questions.explanation: A service account with privileged logon, WMI remote command execution, and admin share access indicates compromised credentials being used for lateral movement.

questions.question: Which MITRE ATT&CK techniques best match the observed activity?
options: T1047 WMI and T1021.002 SMB/Windows Admin Shares
T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service only
questions.explanation: The use of wmic maps to WMI execution, and access to c$ maps to SMB/Windows admin shares.

questions.question: What should be prioritized FIRST?
options: Disable or reset the compromised svc_backup account and isolate involved hosts
Ignore because it is an internal IP
questions.explanation: The privileged account may still be usable, so disabling/resetting it and isolating involved hosts directly limits attacker movement.

questions.question: Which evidence most strongly confirms lateral movement?
options: WMI remote process creation and SMB admin share access
A single DNS query to an internal domain controller
questions.explanation: Remote command execution using WMI and administrative share access are strong indicators of lateral movement.

questions.question: Which log sources would be MOST valuable next?
options: Windows Security logs, Sysmon, PowerShell/WMI logs, and EDR telemetry
questions.explanation: These sources can show logon type, process creation, WMI activity, account usage, and affected hosts.

sections.title: Server Evidence
items: cmd.exe /c whoami executed by web server process
Large outbound HTTPS transfer to a VPS provider

questions.question: What attack chain MOST likely occurred?
options: SQL injection followed by web shell upload and command execution
DNS poisoning followed by DHCP renewal
questions.explanation: The injection payload, successful responses, suspicious upload, command execution, and new admin account indicate SQL injection leading to web shell compromise.

questions.question: Which evidence indicates command execution through the web server?
options: cmd.exe /c whoami executed by the web server process
questions.explanation: The web server process launching operating system commands is direct evidence of server-side command execution.

questions.question: Which evidence suggests persistence?
options: The new local administrator account and retained web shell file
questions.explanation: A new admin account and uploaded web shell can provide continued access after the initial exploit.

questions.question: What should incident responders isolate FIRST?
options: The public website's CSS files only
questions.explanation: The compromised web server is actively executing commands and possibly exfiltrating data, so isolating it limits damage while preserving evidence.

questions.question: Which techniques are MOST relevant?
options: Exploit Public-Facing Application, Web Shell, Create Account, and Exfiltration Over Web Service
questions.explanation: The attack uses a vulnerable web app, web shell execution, account creation for persistence, and outbound transfer that may indicate exfiltration.

title: Insider Data Exfiltration vs Account Compromise

sections.title: DLP Alerts
items: Files uploaded to personal cloud storage

sections.title: Context
items: Employee submitted resignation two days ago
Access originated from normal VPN location

questions.question: What are the TWO most likely explanations?
options: Insider data theft or compromised account activity
DNS cache poisoning or DHCP failure
questions.explanation: Large sensitive downloads, personal cloud upload, resignation, and anomalous timing support either insider threat or compromised account activity.

questions.question: What should analysts verify FIRST?
options: Whether the user had legitimate business justification for the access and upload
Whether the office lights were on
questions.explanation: Before assuming malicious insider behavior, analysts should verify identity, authorization, business need, and whether the activity was legitimate.

questions.question: Which evidence would strengthen insider threat suspicion?
options: Resignation, unusual access time, personal cloud upload, and no malware indicators
questions.explanation: The resignation, timing, and personal cloud upload indicate suspicious user-driven behavior rather than typical automated malware activity.

questions.question: Which containment action is MOST appropriate?
options: Temporarily suspend or restrict the account and block the upload destination while preserving evidence
questions.explanation: Restricting the account and upload path limits further exfiltration while preserving evidence for HR/legal/security review.

questions.question: How should severity generally be classified?
options: High, because sensitive data may have been exfiltrated
Informational, because the VPN country was normal
Low, because no malware was found
No incident, because the employee authenticated successfully
questions.explanation: Potential exfiltration of sensitive engineering data to personal cloud storage is high severity even without malware indicators.

sections.title: EDR Alerts
items: Suspicious process spawned from user Downloads folder

sections.title: Infrastructure Context
items: File server contains shared finance documents
Backups exist but restore status is untested
Several mapped network drives are visible from infected host

questions.question: What activity is MOST likely occurring?
questions.explanation: File renaming, shadow copy deletion, and a suspicious executable are classic indicators of ransomware activity and recovery sabotage.

questions.question: What should be done FIRST?
options: Isolate the infected host from the network
Email all employees the ransom note
questions.explanation: Isolation prevents further encryption across mapped drives and network shares.

questions.question: Why is vssadmin.exe activity significant?
options: It may indicate deletion of shadow copies to prevent easy recovery
questions.explanation: Ransomware commonly deletes volume shadow copies to make recovery harder.

questions.question: What should be verified before restoration?
options: Backup integrity and that backups are not infected/encrypted
questions.explanation: Restoring from compromised or untested backups can reintroduce malware or fail during recovery.

questions.question: Which IR phase includes restoring clean systems from validated backups?
questions.explanation: Recovery focuses on safely restoring business operations after containment and eradication.

sections.title: Company Policy
items: Confidentiality impact is prioritized over availability impact
Public-facing systems are prioritized over internal systems when impact is similar
Exploitability and business criticality must be considered together

sections.title: Vulnerabilities
items: A: External portal, C:H/I:N/A:N, exploit available
B: Internal print server, C:N/I:N/A:H, exploit available
C: Internal wiki, C:L/I:L/A:N, no exploit
D: External marketing site, C:N/I:N/A:L, no exploit

questions.question: Which vulnerability should be remediated FIRST?
options: A: External portal with high confidentiality impact and exploit available
B: Internal print server with high availability impact
C: Internal wiki with low confidentiality and integrity impact
D: External marketing site with low availability impact
questions.explanation: Policy prioritizes confidentiality over availability, and the affected system is external with an available exploit.

questions.question: Why is the internal print server lower priority than the external portal?
options: The policy prioritizes confidentiality and public exposure over internal availability impact
Internal systems do not require patching
questions.explanation: The print server has high availability impact, but the external portal has high confidentiality impact and external exposure.

questions.question: Which factor most increases urgency for Vulnerability A?
options: The presence of a printer queue
questions.explanation: An exploit available against an internet-facing system with high confidentiality impact creates high remediation urgency.

questions.question: What should be considered if immediate patching is impossible?
options: Compensating controls such as WAF rules, access restrictions, or segmentation
questions.explanation: Compensating controls reduce risk when the primary mitigation, such as patching, cannot be applied immediately.

questions.question: What is the best analyst response to conflicting vulnerability priorities?
options: Apply organizational policy and business context to prioritize remediation
Only patch vulnerabilities with short names
questions.explanation: CySA+ vulnerability prioritization requires using policy, exposure, exploitability, and business impact rather than raw severity alone.

sections.title: SOC Metrics
items: Analysts manually close most phishing alerts
Several true positives missed during last month

sections.title: Recent Incident
items: Malware beaconed for 9 days before detection
SIEM generated alerts but analysts ignored them as duplicates

questions.question: What operational failure MOST likely occurred?
options: Alert fatigue caused analysts to miss true positives
questions.explanation: High alert volume, manual closure, missed positives, and burnout indicate alert fatigue and ineffective alert triage.

questions.question: Which improvement would MOST reduce repetitive manual work?
options: SOAR playbooks for duplicate alert handling and enrichment
Reducing log retention to one hour
questions.explanation: SOAR can automate enrichment, deduplication, ticketing, and standardized response steps for repeated alerts.

questions.question: Which SIEM change would improve detection quality?
options: Correlation tuning, baselining, deduplication, and severity adjustment
Alert on every single event equally
questions.explanation: Tuning and baselining reduce noise while preserving meaningful correlations that indicate real attacks.

questions.question: Which metrics should leadership monitor?
options: MTTD, MTTR, false positive rate, alert volume, and missed true positives
Only number of monitors per analyst
questions.explanation: Operational SOC metrics show whether detection and response quality is improving or degrading.

questions.question: What risk does alert fatigue create?
options: True compromises may be ignored or closed as duplicates
questions.explanation: When analysts are overloaded, they may miss or dismiss important alerts, increasing attacker dwell time.

questions.question: What attack activity is MOST likely occurring?
options: Compromised account followed by privilege escalation, persistence, and possible exfiltration
questions.explanation: Successful login, sudo shell, SUID modification, cron job, outbound SSH, and archive creation show a compromise chain beyond normal administration.

questions.question: Why is chmod +s /bin/bash concerning?
options: It can create an SUID root shell privilege escalation path
questions.explanation: Setting the SUID bit on bash can allow users to execute a shell with elevated privileges, depending on system behavior and permissions.

questions.question: Which evidence suggests persistence?
options: Cron job under /etc/cron.d/ and SUID bash modification
questions.explanation: A cron job can re-execute attacker code, and SUID bash can provide a repeated privilege escalation mechanism.

questions.question: Which evidence suggests possible exfiltration?
options: Large archive in /tmp and new outbound SSH connections
questions.explanation: Attackers commonly archive data before transferring it out; outbound SSH connections provide a possible transfer channel.

questions.question: What should be contained FIRST?
options: The affected Linux host and the devops account/session
questions.explanation: Isolating the host and disabling/revoking the compromised account/session limits continued access, privilege escalation, and exfiltration.
